news_summary: Today's news highlights a positive shift toward openly discussing failure in science, fostering transparency and progress in research.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière une évolution positive vers une discussion ouverte sur l'échec dans le domaine scientifique, favorisant ainsi la transparence et le progrès dans la recherche.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy ponen de relieve un cambio positivo hacia un debate abierto sobre el fracaso en el ámbito científico, lo que fomenta la transparencia y el progreso en la investigación.

articles.title: We need to talk about failure in science
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01254-9Failure is part and parcel of research, but discussing it sometimes seems to be taboo in science. It doesn’t need to be.
articles.content:
Failure is part and parcel of research, but discussing it sometimes seems to be taboo in science. It doesn’t need to be.

A failed experiment shouldn’t mean the end of a project, or affect a researcher’s future grant opportunities.Credit: Getty

Last week, Imperial College London, in collaboration with Nature, hosted a conference on a subject that’s rarely talked about in science: failure. The success of a conference on failure didn’t go unremarked, but beyond the meta-humour there was plenty of opportunity for serious discussions.

Science is built on failure in several ways. Scientific ideas and hypotheses need to be tested, refined or rejected to expand humanity’s knowledge. This means that researchers should expect that an experiment or a project might fail, and know how to navigate the consequences. If scientific progress is the practice of scaling the shoulders of giants, let’s not forget that it can be a slippery climb. The Artemis II mission to the Moon, for instance, learnt much from both the successes and the failures of the Apollo missions during the 1960s and 1970s.

In space failure is an option — often the only one

The conference was a welcome and rare occasion to talk about failure in its many guises. A key reason why failure is discussed so little is because of how academic science is structured. Research is funded, communicated and rewarded mainly on the basis of successful results. There is little room in the research system to recognize what might be considered work in progress, or to avoid penalizing people if things go wrong.

This is understandable to some extent. Policymakers increasingly expect that more of taxpayers’ money — distributed as grants by national science funders, for instance — should go to projects that are likely to provide returns on investment; in other words, results. This approach fails to recognize that things can and do go wrong, and that this is part of science, too. It’s important that when experiments or projects fail, researchers investigate why they did and make changes on the basis of what they learnt.

Yet it is becoming harder to implement such changes. On 16 April, the same day as the conference, the European Research Council announced that unsuccessful applicants for its highly prestigious grants are being discouraged from reapplying in the subsequent year. This measure is being introduced to help the organization cope with a rise in applications. But one consequence of the policy shift is that the council is effectively saying to researchers: don’t bother learning from failure, because second chances will be limited.

‘Disruptive’ science has declined — and no one knows why

At Nature, we have always considered appeals to editors’ decisions. More broadly in publishing, things are also starting to change — for example, through innovative publishing formats such as Registered Reports. These articles contain proposals for studies that are peer reviewed and the paper is accepted before the data are collected, promoting methodological rigour instead of focusing on results. But such innovations are still too few and far between. Research papers, grant applications and CVs don’t usually include the experiments or projects that didn’t work out.

This failure of the research enterprise to provide the time and space for individuals to fail without fear of the consequences risks failure of a grander kind. On a broader scale, it might be one reason why studies seem to show that research is becoming less ‘disruptive’ of established ideas (M. Park et al. Nature 613, 138–144; 2023).

Can we learn from research outside academia? In Silicon Valley, for example, failure is famously ‘hard-wired’. A ‘fail fast, fail often’ culture encourages a falsification approach: finding a method or technology that works means experimenting with many things that don’t. Failure is recognized as a necessary experience on the way to success.

But to what extent can such an approach be applied to academic settings? It’s hard to accept that you might fail, or that you are likely to do so, in a culture that rewards only success. Although research is increasingly performed in teams, the overall accountability for obtaining and managing funding, through grants for example, rests mostly with principal investigators.

What elite sport prepared me for in the lab — and what it didn’t

Perhaps we need to look beyond science for inspiration. In a Nature Careers column published on 23 April, Javier Nion Fiera, a PhD student in immunology and microbiology at Louisiana State University in Shreveport, compares his experience in science with his experience in sport (he was a goalkeeper in Spain’s under-19 men’s football league). Athletes are given access to video replays and attend coaching sessions to understand what they could do better, but few mechanisms to assess failure exist in academia. “That uncertainty is what makes scientific failure feel different,” he writes. “Without a clear cause, it’s easy to turn the result inwards. Was it my competence that was to blame? My choice of project? The whole idea?”

There are lessons here for people with managerial responsibilities and for working researchers, too. Scientists can access some support through their university’s internal networks and initiatives, such as Imperial’s Good Science Project, which co-organized the conference. Other institutions will have similar projects. Managers of university training programmes, institutions’ research-support offices and other organizations could all be doing more to help researchers to work through failure.

Principal investigators can also help early-career colleagues to think beyond the idea of ‘standing on the shoulders of giants’ by acknowledging that doubt is “inherent to the scientific profession”, as Marie-Emilie Terret, a cell biologist at the Collège de France in Paris, told Nature in a Careers feature (Nature 651, 543–544; 2026).

In a world of team science, there must be a better way to learn from failure, and not to see it as a burden. We all need to be doing a lot more to make failure a normal part of the scientific process. That begins with talking about it.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a positive cultural shift in science by encouraging open discussion and acceptance of failure as a vital part of the scientific process. This has broad significance for the scientific community and society by potentially improving scientific progress and innovation. The article provides detailed context about current challenges and initiatives, making it a substantive and inspiring call for change.
articles.personality_title: Scientists call for open talks about failure to boost research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Science often focuses on successful results, but failure is a natural part of research. Despite this, talking about experiments or projects that don’t work is rare and sometimes seen as taboo.

**What happened** – Last week, Imperial College London and the journal Nature held a conference to discuss failure in science. Researchers shared ideas about why failure happens, how it affects their work, and why it should be accepted as part of learning and discovery.

**Impact** – This conversation is important because science needs to test ideas, some of which will fail. Without accepting failure, researchers might avoid risky projects or hide their mistakes. The event showed that recognizing failure openly could lead to better understanding and more innovation. For example, space missions like Artemis II learned a lot from past failures to improve future success.

**What's next step** – Some new ways to support failure are appearing, such as publishing study plans before results are known, but more changes are needed. Universities and research leaders can create environments where failure is not punished but studied. This could help scientists try new ideas without fear and improve overall progress.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Talking honestly about failure can help scientists learn more and make stronger discoveries in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques appellent à parler ouvertement de l’échec pour améliorer la recherche
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La science se concentre souvent sur les succès, mais l’échec fait naturellement partie de la recherche. Pourtant, parler des expériences ou projets qui ne fonctionnent pas est rare et parfois tabou.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La semaine dernière, l’Imperial College de Londres et la revue Nature ont organisé une conférence pour parler de l’échec en science. Des chercheurs ont partagé leurs idées sur les raisons des échecs, leur impact, et pourquoi ils doivent être acceptés comme partie de l’apprentissage.

**Impact** – Cette discussion est importante car la science doit tester des idées, dont certaines échouent. Sans accepter l’échec, les chercheurs peuvent éviter les projets risqués ou cacher leurs erreurs. L’événement a montré que reconnaître l’échec ouvertement pourrait mener à plus de compréhension et d’innovation. Par exemple, la mission spatiale Artemis II a beaucoup appris des échecs passés pour réussir.

**Prochaine étape** – De nouvelles façons de soutenir l’échec apparaissent, comme publier des plans d’étude avant les résultats, mais plus de changements sont nécessaires. Les universités et les responsables de la recherche peuvent créer des environnements où l’échec n’est pas puni mais analysé. Cela aiderait les scientifiques à essayer de nouvelles idées sans peur et améliorerait le progrès.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Parler honnêtement de l’échec peut aider les scientifiques à mieux apprendre et faire des découvertes plus solides à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos piden hablar abiertamente sobre el fracaso para mejorar la investigación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La ciencia suele enfocarse en los éxitos, pero el fracaso es parte natural de la investigación. Sin embargo, hablar de experimentos o proyectos que no funcionan es raro y a veces un tabú.

**Qué pasó** – La semana pasada, el Imperial College de Londres y la revista Nature organizaron una conferencia para discutir el fracaso en la ciencia. Investigadores compartieron sus ideas sobre por qué ocurren los fracasos, cómo afectan su trabajo y por qué deben aceptarse como parte del aprendizaje.

**Impacto** – Esta conversación es importante porque la ciencia debe probar ideas, algunas fallan. Sin aceptar el fracaso, los científicos podrían evitar proyectos riesgosos o esconder sus errores. El evento mostró que reconocer el fracaso abiertamente puede llevar a más comprensión e innovación. Por ejemplo, la misión espacial Artemis II aprendió mucho de fracasos pasados para tener éxito.

**Próximo paso** – Aparecen nuevas formas de apoyar el fracaso, como publicar planes de estudio antes de los resultados, pero se necesitan más cambios. Las universidades y líderes de investigación pueden crear ambientes donde el fracaso no se castigue sino se estudie. Esto ayudaría a los científicos a probar nuevas ideas sin miedo y mejoraría el progreso.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Hablar honestamente sobre el fracaso puede ayudar a los científicos a aprender más y hacer descubrimientos más fuertes en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a mountain climber’s silhouette ascending a rugged, steep peak made of layered scientific equipment—test tubes, microscopes, and papers—while scattered broken tools and faded failed experiment notes lie along the path, with soft natural hues emphasizing perseverance and progress through challenges.
